claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in care for 16 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 18, and Blake who is 13.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Since Alex is 18, their situation is particularly important. **Is Alex currently in full-time approved education or training (such as A-levels, NVQs, or an approved training course)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school and aren't doing any courses right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex is 18 and not in approved education or training, they unfortunately won't qualify for Child Benefit. Child Benefit typically stops when a child turns 16, unless they remain in approved education or training (in which case it can continue until age 20).

Now let me ask about Blake. **Does Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, it's a bit complicated. Blake does live with me, but their other parent also lives with Blake and actually already claims Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. That is indeed an important detail.

Just to clarify the living situation: **Do you and Blake's other parent live together in the same household, or do you live separately with Blake splitting time between two homes?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: We live separately. Blake lives with their other parent, and I don't live with Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. So to confirm, Blake primarily lives with their other parent, and their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake.

**Do you ever have Blake staying with you, and if so, how often? For example, do they stay with you on weekends, holidays, or any other regular arrangement?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake does stay with me regularly - they come and stay with me on weekends and some holidays.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to understand the arrangement.

When a child splits their time between two homes, generally only one person can claim Child Benefit for that child. The person who has the child living with them for the majority of the time usually has priority for the claim.

Since Blake's other parent already claims Child Benefit and Blake primarily lives with them (with you having weekend and holiday visits), I have one more question:

**Have you and Blake's other parent been able to agree on who should claim Child Benefit, or is there a dispute about this?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, there's no dispute. Their other parent claims it and that's fine - Blake lives with them most of the time, so that makes sense.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me quickly review the eligibility rules to ensure I provide you with accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for all that information. I now have a clear picture of your situation and can provide you with an eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here is my assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit:

---

**Alex (age 18) – Not Eligible**

Since Alex is 18 and not currently in approved education or training, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for them. Child Benefit normally stops at 16, but can continue until age 20 only if the child stays in approved education or training. If Alex were to return to approved education or training in the future, you may be able to claim at that point.

---

**Blake (age 13) – Not Eligible (for you)**

Although Blake is under 16, they primarily live with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child, and priority is given to the person with whom the child mainly lives. Since Blake lives with their other parent most of the time (with you having weekend and holiday visits), and there is no dispute about the arrangement, you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

---

**Summary**

Unfortunately, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake based on your current circumstances. However, if anything changes in the future—such as Alex returning to approved education, or Blake coming to live with you primarily—you may wish to revisit your eligibility at that time.

I hope this has been helpful. If you have any further questions, please don't hesitate to ask!

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is not currently in approved education or training. Child Benefit normally stops when a child turns 16, unless they remain in approved education or training (in which case it can continue until they turn 20). Since Alex has finished school and is not in any approved education or training, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 13 years old, which would normally qualify for Child Benefit. However, Blake does not live with you - they primarily live with their other parent, and you only have Blake for weekend and holiday visits. Their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. Since Blake's other parent has Blake living with them most of the time and is already receiving Child Benefit, you are not eligible to make a separate claim for Blake. You have also confirmed there is no dispute and you are happy with this arrangement.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (18) and Blake (13). Alex is not eligible because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Blake is not eligible for the claimant because Blake primarily lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can receive Child Benefit per child, and priority goes to the person with whom the child primarily lives.

Since Alex has finished school and is not in any approved education or training, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 13 years old, which would normally qualify for Child Benefit. However, Blake does not live with you - they primarily live with their other parent, and you only have Blake for weekend and holiday visits. Their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child. Since Blake's other parent has Blake living with them most of the time and is already receiving Child Benefit, you are not eligible to make a separate claim for Blake. You have also confirmed there is no dispute and you are happy with this arrangement.

Blake is not eligible for the claimant because Blake primarily lives with their other parent who is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can receive Child Benefit per child, and priority goes to the person with whom the child primarily lives.
